review_question: Does the ACR clearly identify the company/vendor name?
why_this_matters: Without a vendor name, the document is not traceable enough for procurement, renewal, or vendor follow-up.
what_to_look_for: Company, vendor, publisher, or organization name on the cover/title area or report metadata.

review_question: Does the ACR clearly identify the product, platform, or module name?
why_this_matters: The report must identify what product, platform, module, or service the vendor evaluated.
what_to_look_for: Product name, platform name, module, web app, mobile app, hosted service, or interface covered by the report.

review_question: Does the ACR include product version, build, release, or equivalent version information?
why_this_matters: A report tied to an unknown or outdated version may not describe the product currently available to library users.
what_to_look_for: Version number, build number, release version, product edition, platform version, or a clear statement that the product is continuously updated/SaaS.

review_question: Does the ACR include a report date?
why_this_matters: The report date is needed to determine whether the ACR is current or stale.
what_to_look_for: Report date, publication date, evaluation date, last updated date, month/year, or specific date.

review_question: Does the ACR explicitly cover WCAG 2.1 Level A and Level AA?
why_this_matters: For UC Irvine Libraries ADA Title II web/mobile review, the key documentation question is whether WCAG Level A and AA coverage is clearly addressed.
what_to_look_for: Standards/guidelines section, table headings, or ACR metadata showing WCAG 2.1 Level A and Level AA. WCAG 2.2 A/AA is also useful if explicitly stated.

review_question: What VPAT version or edition is used?
why_this_matters: The VPAT version/edition helps staff understand whether the vendor used a recognizable reporting template and what standards are included.
what_to_look_for: VPAT 2.5Rev WCAG, VPAT 2.5Rev INT, VPAT 2.4, VPAT 508, VPAT WCAG, EN 301 549, or similar template/version language.

review_question: Are standard conformance terms used clearly and consistently?
why_this_matters: Staff need clear conformance terms to interpret whether a criterion Supports, Partially Supports, Does Not Support, is Not Applicable, or was Not Evaluated.
what_to_look_for: Supports, Partially Supports, Does Not Support, Not Applicable, Not Evaluated, or equivalent VPAT conformance terms.

review_question: Does the ACR contain an explicit evaluation method?
why_this_matters: Evaluation methods help staff understand whether the ACR is based on manual testing, automated testing, keyboard testing, assistive technology testing, or third-party review.
what_to_look_for: Testing methods, tools, browsers, operating systems, assistive technologies, keyboard testing, mobile testing, test date/period, or third-party auditor scope.

review_question: Were keyboard testing and assistive technology testing mentioned?
why_this_matters: Automated-only testing is weak evidence for keyboard, screen reader, and real user interaction barriers.
what_to_look_for: Keyboard-only testing, NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver, TalkBack, screen magnification, switch access, browsers, operating systems, or mobile assistive technologies.

review_question: What is the conformance result for WCAG 2.1 SC 2.1.1 Keyboard?
why_this_matters: Keyboard access is a core blocker for users who cannot use a mouse and is part of the UC Irvine Libraries critical criteria scan.
what_to_look_for: Locate 2.1.1 Keyboard in the WCAG A/AA table and record the conformance term and remarks.

review_question: What is the conformance result for WCAG 2.1 SC 2.4.7 Focus Visible?
why_this_matters: Visible focus helps keyboard users know where they are on the page and is part of the UC Irvine Libraries critical criteria scan.
what_to_look_for: Locate 2.4.7 Focus Visible in the WCAG A/AA table and record the conformance term and remarks.

review_question: What is the conformance result for WCAG 2.1 SC 1.3.1 Info and Relationships?
why_this_matters: Screen reader users rely on correct structure, labels, headings, tables, and relationships. This criterion is part of the critical criteria scan.
what_to_look_for: Locate 1.3.1 Info and Relationships in the WCAG A/AA table and record the conformance term and remarks.

review_question: What is the conformance result for WCAG 2.1 SC 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value?
why_this_matters: Name, Role, Value is central to assistive technology compatibility for controls, widgets, and dynamic interfaces.
what_to_look_for: Locate 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value in the WCAG A/AA table and record the conformance term and remarks.

review_question: What is the conformance result for WCAG 2.1 SC 3.3.1 Error Identification?
why_this_matters: Users need to know when a form error occurs. This matters for login, requests, booking, account management, and payment workflows.
what_to_look_for: Locate 3.3.1 Error Identification in the WCAG A/AA table and record the conformance term and remarks.

review_question: What is the conformance result for WCAG 2.1 SC 3.3.3 Error Suggestion?
why_this_matters: Users need clear suggestions for fixing errors. This matters for forms, request workflows, account management, booking, and payments.
what_to_look_for: Locate 3.3.3 Error Suggestion in the WCAG A/AA table and record the conformance term and remarks.

review_question: Are the remarks specific enough to understand the accessibility issues?
why_this_matters: Vague or copied remarks make the ACR difficult to use for risk assessment and vendor follow-up.
what_to_look_for: Affected pages, modules, components, examples, limitations, workflows, workaround, remediation status, or target release information.

review_question: Do any known issues appear to affect common library workflows?
why_this_matters: A single barrier in login, search, reading content, request submission, booking, account, payment, or help tools can be higher risk than several minor issues elsewhere.
what_to_look_for: Look for remarks tied to login/account, discovery/search, reading/viewing/downloading content, requests/holds, reservations/bookings, payments/fees, forms, or help/chat tools.

review_question: If remediation is mentioned, is it specific and trackable?
why_this_matters: A remediation promise is only useful if it says what will be fixed and when, such as a specific date, target quarter/year, target month/year, or release version with timeline.
what_to_look_for: ACR remarks, vendor roadmap, release notes, vendor email, target date, target quarter/year, target month/year, or target release version.

review_question: What should library staff ask the vendor next?
why_this_matters: The app should help non-experts take the next practical step, not just produce a technical finding.
what_to_look_for: Missing ACR fields, unclear WCAG coverage, vague remarks, missing evaluation methods, common workflow impact, or missing remediation dates.
